coredata.dc:title: A molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor based on upconversion nanoparticles enhanced by electrodeposited rGO for selective and ultrasensitive detection of clenbuterol
coredata.dc:creator: Jin, Xincui
Fang, Guozhen
Pan, Mingfei
Yang, Yukun
Bai, Xiaoyun
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: A simple, efficient and sensitive molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor (MIECLS) based on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) was developed for determination of clenbuterol (CLB). In this study, rGO generated by electrodeposition of graphene oxide not only acted as carrier for immobilizing UCNPs, but also had a significant impact in boosting electrochemiluminescence (ECL) response of UCNPs thanks to its high conductivity, superior electron transport rate and large specific surface area. UCNPs as an advanced ECL emitter possessed wonderful ECL performance. Furthermore, the introduction of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) endowed the ECL sensor a new character of specifically identifying analyte CLB. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the ECL signal was proportional to the logarithm of CLB concentration in the range of 10nM to 100µM with a low detection limit of 6.3nM. The proposed MIECLS combining the advantages of UCNPs-ECL and MIP exhibited good sensitivity, desirable selectivity and favorable stability, indicating enormous potential in the future of food safety detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence sensor
Reduced graphene oxide
Upconversion nanoparticles
Molecularly imprinted polymers
Clenbuterol

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast doctopic grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Surface morphology of the modified electrodes
ECL and EIS characterization of various modified electrodes
ECL mechanism and the quenching effect of CLB towards MIECLS
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of MIECLS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of rGO/GCE (A), UCNPs/GCE (B), UCNPs/rGO/GCE (C) and MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE (D). TEM image of UCNPs (E).

(A) ECL curves of bare GCE (a), rGO/GCE (b), UCNPs/GCE (c), UCNPs/rGO/GCE (d) in 100mmolL−1 K2S2O8 solution (PBS, 0.1molL−1, pH 7.4). (B) ECL curves of MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE before (a), after (b) the elution of CLB and after incubation (c) in 5 × 10−6 molL−1 CLB, NIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE before (d) and after (e) elution. EIS of (C) bare GCE (a), rGO/GCE (b), UCNPs/GCE (c), UCNPs/rGO/GCE (d) and (D) MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE before (a), after (b) the elution of CLB, and after incubation (c) in a solution containing 5mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6]/K4[Fe(CN)6] and 0.2molL−1 KNO3. (E) PL spectrum of UCNPs obtained with excitation wavelength 980nm (a) and ECL spectrum of UCNPs/GCE (b). (F) UV–vis absorption spectra of 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 (a), 1 × 10−4 molL−1 CLB (b), 1 × 10−4 molL−1 CLB + 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 (c).

(A) ECL responses of the constructed MIECLS with various CLB concentrations: 0, 1 × 10−8, 5 × 10−8, 1 × 10−7, 5 × 10−7, 1 × 10−6, 5 × 10−6, 1 × 10−5, 5 × 10−5, 1 × 10−4 molL−1. (B) Calibration curve of MIECLS for CLB detection. (C) Selectivity of the MIECLS to CLB and its structural analogs with the same concentration. (D) ECL stability of MIECLS with 1µmolL−1 CLB in PBS (0.1molL−1, pH 7.4) containing 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8.
(A) The detailed preparation process of MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE. (B) ECL mechanism and the quenching mechanism of CLB towards MIECLS.
Comparison of this method with other methods reported in the literatures.
Determination of CLB in pig samples by the proposed MIECLS and HPLC.

head.ce:title: A molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor based on upconversion nanoparticles enhanced by electrodeposited rGO for selective and ultrasensitive detection of clenbuterol

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Science, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Science, Shanxi University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology & Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Food Nutrition and Human Health, Beijing Technology & Business University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple, efficient and sensitive molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor (MIECLS) based on reduced graphene oxide (rGO) and upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) was developed for determination of clenbuterol (CLB). In this study, rGO generated by electrodeposition of graphene oxide not only acted as carrier for immobilizing UCNPs, but also had a significant impact in boosting electrochemiluminescence (ECL) response of UCNPs thanks to its high conductivity, superior electron transport rate and large specific surface area. UCNPs as an advanced ECL emitter possessed wonderful ECL performance. Furthermore, the introduction of molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) endowed the ECL sensor a new character of specifically identifying analyte CLB. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the ECL signal was proportional to the logarithm of CLB concentration in the range of 10nM to 100µM with a low detection limit of 6.3nM. The proposed MIECLS combining the advantages of UCNPs-ECL and MIP exhibited good sensitivity, desirable selectivity and favorable stability, indicating enormous potential in the future of food safety detection.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an optical radiation from the excited state produced by high energy electron transfer of electrochemically generated reagents at electrode surface (Miao, 2008; Wu et al., 2014b). ECL has been widely applied in various fields, including bioscience, immunoassay and food analysis due to its advantages of rapidity, low background noise, high sensitivity and simplified equipment (Jiang and Wang, 2009; Yang et al., 2015). For ECL emitters, great interest was focused on ruthenium (Ⅱ) complexes and luminol at the early development of ECL and later much attention was turned to nanomaterials, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots, graphite-like carbon nitride (g-C3N4) and noble metal clusters (Fan et al., 2016; Li et al., 2012, 2014a; Lou et al., 2015). It will be an irresistible trend to devote to exploring and studying new and efficient ECL materials.

Rare earth doped upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs) following anti-Stokes’ law can absorb two or more low-energy photons to emit high-energy photons. In recent years, the applications of UCNPs in the fields of bioimaging, analytical testing and clinical medicine have been immensely reported thanks to their good emission stability and fine biocompatibility (Liu et al., 2014; Pokhrel et al., 2014). Nevertheless, the research of UCNPs is rarely reported in ECL field. UCNPs as a new generation of ECL luminescent reagents possess a number of potential advantages such as stable cathode signals, desirable ECL emission and prolonged fluorescence lifetime and thus will expand application scope of ECL (Guo et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2012). Ren's group first reported the application of UCNPs in ECL investigation in 2012 and they made use of one-step synthesis of graphene-upconversion composites to construct an ECL sensor, in which graphene played an important role in amplifying ECL signal (Yin et al., 2012). Graphene, as a two-dimensional material with single atom structure, shows attractive application prospects in multifarious fields (Kim et al., 2009). The outstanding characteristics of graphene such as large specific surface area, low cost and high electron transfer speed, make it possible to be used for ECL signal amplification (Shan et al., 2009; Pumera, 2011; Zhao et al., 2016a). Reduced graphene oxide (rGO) can be obtained by electrodeposition of graphene oxide (GO) and rGO film yielded by this way not only can be controlled in thickness and size, but also possesses uniformity and continuity (Liu et al., 2011a). However, the method using rGO produced by electrochemical reduction to enhance ECL signal of UCNPs has not been explored and thus it will manifest enormous potential in fabricating a novel and sensitive ECL sensor.

Molecular imprinting technique (MIT) is a process to prepare polymers with recognition sites having a specific selectivity for template molecules (Wu et al., 2012). The resulting molecularly imprinted polymers (MIP) have been extensively utilized for sensors due to superb recognition capability and desirable stability. Molecularly imprinted electrochemiluminescence sensor (MIECLS) is the ideal integration of ECL and MIP, and hence MIECLS not only maintains good sensitivity and controllability inherited from ECL, but also shows predominant selectivity of MIP (Yang et al., 2017). However, MIECLS using UCNPs as luminophor remains unexplored.

Clenbuterol (CLB) as adrenaline nervous stimulant plays a positive role in clinical application of treating bronchitis and myasthenia gravis (Lichtenstein and Deckelbaum, 2001). It can also promote animal growth and improve lean percentage. Whereas CLB is easily absorbed by animal body and a large number of trials have shown that CLB residues mainly exist in eyes, liver, kidney and muscle (Zhao et al., 2011). In recent years, CLB is frequently added to animal feed and its abuse has caused lots of incidents of human poisoning (Yan et al., 2014). As a result, many countries including China have banned the use of CLB as animal growth promoter.

Nowadays a number of methods have been developed for CLB detection, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) (Zhao et al., 2010), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chang et al., 2005), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) (Lu et al., 2013), capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Wang et al., 2015) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Ren et al., 2009). GC-MS and LC-MS methods possess high sensitivity, but they need expensive instruments and time-consuming sample pretreatment. Although ELISA method is simple and fast, a primary drawback is high expense of producing antibodies.

In this work, a novel and ultrasensitive MIECLS was exploited for the detection of CLB. GO was electrochemically reduced on electrode surface to generate rGO, and then the resulting rGO as signal amplifying agent and UCNPs as luminophor were used for fabricating an ECL sensor. Driven by the electrostatic interaction, positively charged UCNPs and negatively charged rGO can be assembled together making ECL signal more stable. And rGO by virtue of its super properties of wonderful conductivity and excellent electron transport can tremendously boost ECL intensity of UCNPs and improve ECL performance of UCNPs. Besides, the ECL response of UCNPs/rGO modified electrode gradually reduced with increasing CLB concentration and the possible ECL mechanism and quenching mechanism were explored in detail. MIP was further introduced to the ECL sensor based on rGO and UCNPs to specifically recognize template CLB and endowed the ECL sensor with good selectivity realizing quantitative determination for CLB. Therefore, the proposed MIECLS with stable and strong cathodic ECL signal would provide a new way for CLB detection in food safety analysis.

Carboxyl graphene oxide dispersion (GO, 2mgmL−1) and SiO2-NH2 modified upconversion nanoparticles (NaYF4:Yb,Er) were supplied by XFNANO (Nanjing, China). O-phenylenediamine (o-PD, 99.5%) was supplied by J&K Scientific Ltd. (Beijing, China). Isoprenaline (ISO, 99.2%), salbutamol (SAL, 99.7%) and terbutaline (TER, 99.8%) were purchased from Chinese Food and Drug Inspection Institute. CLB (95%) and potassium peroxodisulfate (K2S2O8) were achieved from Sigma-Aldrich. Ractopamine (RAC, 97.0%) was purchased from Dr. Ehrenstorfer GmbH (Germany). Millipore system purified water was used in all of the experiments.

All ECL experiments were tested with a LK5100 ECL analyzer (Tianjin Lanlike Chemical and Electronic High Technology Co., Ltd., China). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was taken by a PARSTAT 2273 electrochemical workstation. All of the above measurements employed a typical three-electrode system, which used a glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 4mm), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) and a platinum wire electrode as working, reference and auxiliary electrodes, respectively. Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) spectra were measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo, America). Fluorescence spectra were obtained on an F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) with an external 980nm exciter. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), which was used to track the surface morphologies of the modified electrodes, was performed on a LEO-1530VP scanning electron microscopy (LEO, Germany). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Japan) was carried out on a JEM-2010FEF transmission electron microscopy to observe the structure morphology of the nanomaterials.

GO (2mgmL−1) was diluted into 0.5mgmL−1 with deionized water under sonication for 10min. UCNPs (2mgmL−1) were also dispersed in aqueous solution to the final concentration of 0.5mgmL−1. The bare GCE was successively polished with 1.0, 0.3 and 0.05µm alumina slurry to a smooth plane followed by rinsing entirely with deionized water and then measured with cyclic voltammetry in 1mmolL−1 K3[Fe(CN)6] containing 0.2molL−1 KNO3 until the peak potential difference was below 80mV. The resulting electrode was immersed in the above GO dispersion for electrodeposition from 0.6 to −1.4V with a scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 5 cycles to obtain a rGO modified electrode (rGO/GCE). Subsequently, 10µL UCNPs solution (0.5mgmL−1) was dropped on rGO/GCE surface and dried at room temperature to obtain a UCNPs/rGO modified electrode (UCNPs/rGO/GCE).

The CLB-imprinted UCNPs/rGO/GCE was prepared in the deoxygenated acetate buffer (0.1molL−1, pH 5.2) consisting of 6mmolL−1 o-PD and 1.2mmolL−1 CLB under darkness using cyclic voltammetry from 0 to 0.8V with a scan rate of 50mVs−1 for 7 cycles. The obtained electrode was placed in 25mL methanol/acetic (2:1, v/v) solution with continuous magnetic stirring for 20min to remove the embedded template and subsequently rinsed with deionized water and allowed to dry naturally. The NIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE (non-imprinted polymers, NIP) as a control experiment was also prepared with the same electro-polymerization process as the MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE but no template CLB. The fabrication procedure of MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE was illustrated in Scheme 1 (A).

The MIECLS was immersed in various concentrations of CLB to adsorb template molecules for 6min and then detected in 5mL 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 solution containing 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4). In the process of ECL emission, cyclic voltammetry and electrochemiluminescence were simultaneously adopted. And the voltage of photomultiplier tube was placed at 800V, meanwhile cathodic potential ranged from 0 to −2.5V with a scan rate of 100mVs−1. EIS performed from 100mHz to 100kHz with AC amplitude of 10mV was used for characterization of different modified electrodes.

In the experiment, three kinds of samples including pork, pig liver and pig kidney purchased from a local supermarket were chosen for spiked recovery experiments to evaluate the practicality of established MIECLS for trace analysis of CLB. After being minced with a blender, the samples (10 ± 0.01g) were placed into a 50mL glass centrifuge tube and spiked with three concentrations (0.200, 0.500, 1.000mgkg−1) of CLB solution and then placed overnight under dark. After that, 15mL 0.1molL−1 perchloric acid (HClO4) was added followed by sonication for 20min and then processed by heating at 80°C for 30min. After cooling and centrifugation at 4500rpm for 15min, the supernatant was decanted to another 50mL glass centrifuge tube and the precipitation was centrifuged again with 10mL HClO4. Then the supernatants of the two processes were collected and the pH was adjusted to 9.5 ± 0.1 with NaOH (1molL−1). Next, 8g NaCl and 25mL isopropyl alcohol/ethyl acetate (2:3, v/v) were added to extract the mixture and then the organic phase was transferred to a rotary vial. The residue was extracted again with 20mL isopropyl alcohol/ethyl acetate (2:3, v/v). The organic phase was combined and concentrated to dryness at 60°C. At last, the residue was sufficiently dissolved with 2mL 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4), filtered with a 0.22µm microporous membrane for ECL detection.

For HPLC detection, the above residue was dissolved with 0.1molL−1 sodium dihydrogen phosphate buffer (NaH2PO4, 1mL, pH 6.0), filtered with a 0.22µm microporous membrane into a 5mL volumetric flask and diluted to scale with 0.1molL−1 NaH2PO4 (pH 6.0). Moreover, appropriate amount of the above extract was added to the weak cation exchange column (LC-WCX) which was activated with ethanol (10mL), water (3mL), NaH2PO4 (3mL, 0.1molL−1, pH 6.0) buffer and water (3mL) before. After rinsing the LC-WCX with water (4mL) and ethanol (4mL), the effluent was discarded. The LC-WCX was subsequently rinsed with 6mL ethanol/ammonia (98:2, v/v) and the resulting effluent was collected. Finally, the collection was concentrated to dryness under nitrogen-evaporator and redissolved with 2mL methanol/water (45:55, v/v).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology of the modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies of prepared electrodes were observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, the GCE surface was covered with a layer of uniform film, presenting flakelike shape and wrinkle texture structure after the electrodeposition of GO, which was consistent with the reported literature (Shao et al., 2010). Fig. 1B indicated UCNPs neatly coated the electrode surface. Simultaneously, the TEM image of Fig. 1E (inset) showed UCNPs presented circle and core-shell structure and the size was uniform with the particle size of approximately 35nm. According to Fig. 1C, UCNPs could be seen to be tightly bonded to the surface of rGO due to the unique pleated structure and large specific surface area of rGO increasing the contact between UCNPs and rGO. Fig. 1D showed that UCNPs/rGO/GCE surface was covered with MIP. The outline of UCNPs could be seen and large particles existed comparing with UCNPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and EIS characterization of various modified electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: ECL profiles in Fig. 2A showed the characterization of bare GCE (a), rGO/GCE (b), UCNPs/GCE (c), and UCNPs/rGO/GCE (d). From curve (a), it was observed that bare GCE only had a weak ECL intensity (~ 1715a.u.). When rGO was modified on bare GCE, the ECL intensity (~ 6233a.u.) increased a bit which could be attributed to the superior conductivity of rGO increasing the ability of electron transfer. As shown in curve (c) the ECL intensity of UCNPs/GCE (~ 16473a.u.) was almost 9.6-fold as high as that of bare GCE, suggesting that UCNPs as luminophor had excellent luminous performance. As shown in curve (d), the ECL intensity improved significantly presenting the highest ECL emission (~ 56542a.u.) after the introduction of rGO to UCNPs/GCE. Generally, rGO obtained by electrodeposition of GO on GCE surface possessed favorable conductivity, excellent electrochemical activity and good stability and it could amplify the ECL signal of UCNPs (Sakda et al., 2016). Moreover, rGO owned large specific surface area allowing UCNPs and rGO to be sufficiently contacted, and positively charged UCNPs could quickly adhere to the surface of negatively charged rGO by electrostatic interaction (Kochmann et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2012b). Therefore, it was able to see the ECL intensity of UCNPs/rGO/GCE was 33.0-fold that of bare GCE and 3.4-fold that of UCNPs/GCE.

EIS as a way of analyzing electrode process kinetics and studying electrode materials was further used for the characterization of various modified electrodes. It could be seen from Fig. 2C, rGO/GCE exhibited the smallest charge transfer resistance (R ct) of 208Ω (far below 415Ω of bare GCE) which further illustrated that rGO was a predominant conductive material with extraordinary electron transport capacity and could accelerate electron transfer rate of the ECL process. Because UCNPs were modified with a layer of silica which possessed poor conductivity hindering the electron transfer, the R ct of UCNPs/GCE showed a large semicircle (Liu et al., 2014). However, compared with UCNPs/GCE (~ 748Ω), the R ct of UCNPs/rGO/GCE about 251Ω was obviously lower, implying that rGO improved the electrical conductivity of UCNPs and also facilitated electron transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL mechanism and the quenching effect of CLB towards MIECLS
ce:section.ce:para:
PL and ECL spectra of UCNPs were measured. To our best knowledge, the e, and a red emission at 658 nm attributinmission spectrum of rare earth ions is mainly caused by the transition of inner layer 4f electrons. It could be seen from Fig. 2E inset UCNPs had a green emission at 525 and 543nm owing to 2H11/2 → 4I15/2 and 4S3/2 → 4I15/2 transition of Er3+, respectively, and a red emission at 658nm attributing to 4F9/2 → 4I15/2 transition of Er3+ (Zhao et al., 2016a). As shown in Fig. 2E, ECL spectrum of UCNPs achieving maximum at 555nm presented a little red shift comparing with PL spectrum with maximum at 543nm, which may be attributed to the distinction of their light-emitting mechanisms. For ECL process, the possible mechanism may be interpreted by electron transfer (Yin et al., 2012). When the electrode was scanned with an initial negative potential, the NaYF4:Yb,Er immobilized on the electrode surface was first reduced to negatively charged radical (NaYF4:Yb,Erˉ•) by charge injection. Simultaneously, the coreactant S2O8 2- in solution diffusing to the electrode surface was electrochemically reduced to the strong oxidative anion sulfate radical (SO4ˉ•) which could further react with NaYF4:Yb,Erˉ• to produce excited state NaYF4:Yb,Er* and then NaYF4:Yb,Er* returned to ground state to release photons and generate light. Therefore, according to the above discussion, the ECL mechanism was speculated as follows (Wu et al., 2014a; Yin et al., 2012):
(1) NaYF4:Yb,Er + eˉ → NaYF4:Yb,Erˉ•
(2) S2O8 2- + eˉ → SO4 2- + SO4ˉ•
(3) NaYF4:Yb,Erˉ• + SO4ˉ• → NaYF4:Yb,Er* + SO4 2-
(4) NaYF4:Yb,Er* → NaYF4:Yb,Er + hv

After MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE being incubated in CLB solution at a certain concentration, the ECL intensity decreased, because the recombination of target CLB with the cavities on the molecularly imprinted membrane (MIM) could block electron transport channels between luminophor UCNPs and coreactant K2S2O8. In addition, as illustrated in Fig. 3A, the ECL signal gradually reduced with the increasing concentration of CLB. The possible quenching mechanism of CLB towards UCNPs was speculated as follows. CLB could be oxidized by strong oxidizing SO4ˉ•, which would be further certified by Fig. 2F. According to the UV–vis spectra, curve b showed three typical UV absorption peaks belonging to CLB located at 215, 243 and 293nm, respectively, whereas when CLB was simply mixed with K2S2O8, the absorption peak changed greatly (curve c) and the characteristic peaks of CLB disappeared indicating the production of new intermediates, and it was deduced that the new intermediate could quench the ECL intensity of UCNPs (Xia et al., 2016; Zhao et al. 2016b). Based on the above discussion, the oxidation quenching mechanism was further demonstrated. Besides, the quenching mechanism may also be interpreted as the consumption of SO4ˉ• due to the oxidation of CLB by SO4ˉ• (Chen et al., 2015; Wang et al., 2012a). As exhibited in Scheme 1 (B), the possible quenching detection mechanism was displayed according to the above demonstration and previous reports.

It is quite important to consider multiple factors in the process of constructing MIECLS to obtain the optimal ECL performance. The number of electrodeposition cycles for GO affects the ECL enhancement of UCNPs to a certain extent and appropriate electrodeposition amount of GO can provide a certain adsorption sites for UCNPs and effectively accelerate electron transfer rate, thus making the ECL intensity of UCNPs reach the optimum. And the results in Fig. S1 showed that the ECL intensity of UCNPs did not change significantly after 5 cycles of electrodeposition, therefore 5 cycles were selected as the optimal electrodeposition cycle.

In the preparation process of molecular imprinting membranes, the ratio of template and monomer, thickness of the film, the type and proportion of eluent, and elution and incubation time are crucial factors. The MIP-based sensors fabricated by different ratio of template and monomer (1:1, 1:3, 1:5, 1:7, 1:9, the concentration of o-PD was 6mmolL−1) were tested after adsorbing the same concentration of CLB (50µmolL−1) and when the ratio was 1:5, the ECL quenching value was maximum (Fig. S2(A)). Hence, the optimal ratio of template and monomer was chosen to be 1:5. Thickness of the MIM, which can affect the response signal of MIECLS and the diffusion rate of CLB and K2S2O8 into MIM, can be adjusted by changing the number of polymerization cycles. When the number of polymerization cycles was small, the film was too thin to be stable, but when the number of polymerization cycles was large, the film was so thick that it was difficult for CLB and K2S2O8 to diffuse into the MIM. As shown in Fig. S2(B), the ECL quenching value reached maximum at 7 cycles. The eluent used for removing the template CLB to obtain imprinted pores which can specifically recognize the target is an extremely critical factor. Eluent with different sorts and proportions was explored in the experiment and the results in Fig. S2(C) indicated methanol/acetic acid (2:1, v/v) as eluent could quickly and effectively remove template making the ECL quenching value reach maximum. Furthermore, elution and incubation time were studied in the experiment. Fig. S2(D) (curve a) showed that the relationship of ECL intensity and elution time, and with the increase of elution time the ECL intensity gradually increased in 20min and then reached a steady value after 20min. Hence, 20min was chosen as the optimal elution time. Fig. S2(D) (curve b) showed that with the increase of incubation time, the ECL intensity rapidly reduced in 6min and maintained almost stable after 6min, therefore the optimal incubation time was chosen to be 6min.

Besides, the response of MIECLS is also influenced by pH of the detection solution. It could be found from Fig. S3 the ECL quenching value increased with the pH varying from 5.4 to 7.4, and then started to decrease when the pH was over 7.4. So pH 7.4 as the optimum was used for later experiments.

To demonstrate the superior performance of MIP-based ECL sensor, the non-imprinted UCNPs/rGO/GCE and CLB-imprinted UCNPs/rGO/GCE were both detected by ECL measurement in 0.1molL−1 K2S2O8 solution containing 0.1molL−1 PBS (pH 7.4). It could be observed from Fig. 2B the ECL curve of CLB-imprinted UCNPs/rGO/GCE showed an extremely weak ECL peak (curve a), which was similar to that of non-imprinted UCNPs/rGO/GCE (inset, curve d). This may be due to the fact that UCNPs/rGO/GCE was covered with nonconductive polymer of MIM or non-imprinted membrane (NIM) which could hinder the electron transfer between luminophor UCNPs and co-reactant S2O8 2- resulting in a sharp decrease of ECL intensity. However, when the template was removed from the MIM, the ECL intensity evidently restored (curve b) because the formed cavities provided channels for electron transfer between UCNPs and S2O8 2- and thus accelerated electron transport. Compared with MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE, the ECL emission of NIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE (curve e) restored only a little due to the lack of cavities after elution. As shown in curve c, after MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE was incubated in CLB solution of 5µM, the ECL intensity decreased again because the blocked cavities by CLB could suppress electron transfer rate and meanwhile target CLB played a vital role in inhibiting luminous efficiency of UCNPs.

Besides, EIS was further used for characterization of MIP-based ECL sensor to indicate the successful construction and predominant properties of the sensor. As can be seen from Fig. 2D, the R ct of MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE showed a very large semicircle (curve a), implying the nonconductivity of MIM. But after template CLB was removed from MIM, the R ct of the modified electrode obviously decreased (curve b) owing to the formation of cavities making electron transfer between UCNPs and S2O8 2- easier. And after template CLB recombined with MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE, a part of cavities were blocked making it difficult to transfer electron and thus the R ct became large again (curve c).

The relationship between ECL response value and CLB concentration was shown in Fig. 3A and it could be found that the ECL signal gradually decreased with increasing concentration of CLB. Additionally, under the optimal experimental conditions, ECL quenching value/initial value (F 0-F/F 0) was linearly related to the logarithm of CLB concentration within a certain range from 10nM to 100µM and the linear correlation coefficient (R 2) was 0.9957 (n = 9, Fig. 3B). The linear regression equation was shown as follows:
F
0
− F /
F
0
= 0.1367
lg
C
CLB
+ 1.1311

Furthermore, the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 6.3nM (S/N = 3). Compared with other detection measures ( Table 1), the fabricated MIECLS in this study was time-saving and possessed low detection limit, wide linear range and good reproducibility, further indicating that the MIECLS presented excellent properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of MIECLS
ce:section.ce:para: It is well-known that MIT has excellent specificity and selectivity and template molecules can specifically combine with recognition sites in imprinted polymers. And a selective experiment was carried out to evaluate the recognition capability of proposed MIECLS. CLB and its structural analogs including salbutamol (a), ractopamine (b), terbutaline (c), isoproterenol (d) (shown in Fig. S4) at the same concentration of 1µM were tested, respectively, and the results were shown in Fig. 3C. Because imprinted cavities of MIM can perfectly match with spatial structure and binding sites of the template CLB, the ECL quenching value of CLB was significantly higher than that of other four analogs indicating the constructed MIECLS exhibited selectivity towards CLB.

Stability and reproducibility were nonnegligible properties for evaluating the performance of MIECLS. Fig. 3D showed the ECL intensity of MIECLS under continuous cyclic potential scans for 11 cycles and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of ECL signal for 1µM CLB was 2.08% indicating outstanding stability and reliability. In order to evaluate the reproducibility of MIECLS, five pretreated MIP/UCNPs/rGO/GCE were measured after incubating at 1 × 10−6 molL−1 CLB, and the RSD of ECL quenching value was 4.6%. The regeneration of MIECLS was also evaluated. The MIP-based sensor was continuously tested five times after elution with methanol/acetic acid (2:1, v/v) for 20min and readsorption with 5µM CLB for 6min every time. The results in Fig. S5 revealed that the ECL quenching value presented a slight decline, implying the prepared sensor in this work could be recycled several times.

In order to demonstrate the applicability and feasibility of the established MIECLS, different concentrations of CLB in pig samples including pork, liver and kidney were tested by recovery experiments. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries for CLB detection in real samples employing MIECLS ranged from 89.0% to 100.4% with RSDs below 4.8%. Furthermore, traditional HPLC method was employed to validate the accuracy of proposed MIECLS for detection results. As can be seen from Table 2, there was no significant difference between the detection results of MIECLS and HPLC method. Also, statistical analysis was carried out to confirm the consistency between the established method and HPLC for sample determination. The results indicated that there was a good correlation between MIECLS and HPLC method with R 2 of 0.9989, suggesting the prepared MIECLS can be used for practical analysis of CLB in real samples.

In this study, UCNPs as a novel electrochemical luminescence material were used to construct ECL sensor for sensitive and rapid detection of CLB. Meanwhile, the introduction of rGO by electrodeposition to the ECL sensor significantly amplified the ECL signal of UCNPs due to the excellent electrochemical properties and wonderful electroconductivity of rGO and the ECL intensity of UCNPs/rGO/GCE was 3.4-fold that of UCNPs/GCE, further verifying the significance of rGO. Then a MIECLS was established based on rGO enhanced ECL to selectively recognize CLB. The prepared MIECLS possessed quicker response, higher sensitivity, wider response range and lower detection limit comparing with other detection methods for CLB reported in literatures. Furthermore, the sensor in this work had a great many other features, such as favorable stability and superior reproducibility making it possible to be used for practical application.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Tianjin Municipal Science and Technology Commission, the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFD0401204), Ministry of Science and Technology of China (No. 2012BAK17B03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.11.016.
